behaviors.description: Invalid request signature is rejected with 401; body is not forwarded to queue
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is a pure cryptographic operation. The failure mode is: code fails to reject invalid signatures OR queues the event despite invalid signature. Both are catchable by mocking the queue and verifying the response code and queue non-invocation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the queue client. Make a request with X-Signature-256 that doesn't match the body. Assert response is 401 and queue.enqueue() was never called.

behaviors.description: Event is persisted to queue before HTTP 200 acknowledgment returns to sender
behaviors.justification: This tests causal ordering and timing: the enqueue must complete before the response is sent. A synchronous mock would hide bugs where the code sends response before enqueue completes, or where enqueue is fire-and-forget async. A unit test with clever spies might catch it, but the failure is subtle (promise timing). A realistic test needs to observe that response returns only after queue write completes.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real queue or test double with observable latency. Inject timing observers on both queue.enqueue() and response.send(). Send a request and verify that queue write happens before or during response transmission, not after. Alternatively, mock queue with a promise that doesn't resolve until verified in test, and verify response doesn't return until enqueue promise resolves.

behaviors.description: Handler returns HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing to finish
behaviors.justification: Tests that downstream processing is async and non-blocking. A unit test with a synchronous mock would pass even if the code incorrectly awaits downstream completion. The failure mode requires observing that the response is returned before downstream processing completes. This needs real async execution or a sophisticated async test double that can report when processing is still in-flight.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real downstream service or async test double. Trigger downstream processing that takes >500ms. Verify the HTTP response returns within 50ms (before processing completes). Assert downstream is still executing after response is sent.

behaviors.description: Response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when prior events have slow downstream processing
behaviors.justification: Tests concurrent request handling and response-time SLA under load. The failure mode is queue blocking or synchronous waiting. A unit test with synchronous mocks would hide queue-blocking bugs. This requires real async concurrency, actual timing measurements, and multiple in-flight requests. Wall-clock timing is inherently non-unit-testable.
behaviors.test_description: Send request A with downstream processing that takes 5 seconds. Immediately send request B. Measure wall-clock time from request B submission to response receipt. Assert <3 seconds. Verify B's response is independent of A's downstream completion.

behaviors.description: Duplicate event IDs are not enqueued a second time
behaviors.justification: Tests idempotency / dedup logic. The failure mode is: handler enqueues the same ID twice. This could be caught at Unit level if dedup is simple in-memory logic (test by sending same event twice and checking queue mock), but given the system emphasizes robustness and persistence, dedup state likely lives in a database or queue. Mocking the dedup check would hide bugs in the persistent dedup storage itself. Integration test verifies the actual dedup mechanism works.
behaviors.test_description: Send an event with ID='abc123' and verify it's enqueued. Send the same event again. Verify queue.enqueue() is called only once, or use a real dedup store and verify the second write is rejected.

behaviors.description: Handler returns 503 with Retry-After header when queue reports it is full
behaviors.justification: Tests the handler's response to a queue error signal. The failure mode is: handler doesn't check for full status or returns wrong status code. A mock queue can easily simulate a 'full' error or exception. No need for real queue behavior—just the handler's error handling logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the queue client to throw an error (e.g., QueueFullError or return {full: true}). Make a request. Assert response is 503 and includes Retry-After header with a valid value.

behaviors.description: Persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that exceed it
behaviors.justification: Tests the queue subsystem's enforcement of capacity limits. The failure mode is: queue doesn't respect the configured limit, or misconfiguration allows overflow. A mock queue can always succeed, completely hiding this failure. This requires a real queue instance (or highly realistic test double) that actually enforces capacity. The test is about the queue's real behavior, not just the handler's response to an error.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real queue (local database, in-process queue, or test container) configured with capacity=N. Enqueue N events successfully. Attempt to enqueue event N+1 and verify it's rejected. Alternatively, fill the queue to capacity and assert that further enqueue attempts fail.
